metadata.title: The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication: Evidence from a correlated topic model
keywords: C32 C82 D72 D83 E61 Tax policy U

abstract:
We combine a probabilistic topic model and a dictionary-based sentiment analysis to construct a time series, which indicates when and how (positive vs. negative) the U.S. president communicates his tax policy news to the public. The econometric analyses show that optimistic tax policy statements stimulate consumption, investment, and output, even after controlling for tax foresight. We also find that consumer sentiment reacts positively to more optimistic tax news, suggesting that sentiment plays an important role in the transmission from U.S. presidential tax policy communication to economic activity.

☆ We would like to thank two anonymous referees, Thomas Stratmann, Bernd Kempa, Bernd Wilfling, Nicole Branger, and the participants of the 9th International Conference on Computational and Financial Econometrics for helpful comments and valuable discussions. We also thank Todd B. Walker for providing the data for the implicit tax rate.

sections.paper_id: Dybowski and Adämmer - 2018 - The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication Evidence from a correlated topic model
paragraphs: Economic research has shown that presidential announcements on future tax changes can lead to shifts in expectations, affecting economic activity well ahead of the actual policy implementation (see e.g., Mertens and Ravn, 2011;Mertens and Ravn, 2012;Leeper et al., 2012;Leeper et al., 2013b). Aside from this expectation channel, presidential tax policy speeches may give rise to ancillary sentiment effects that feed into economic activity. Yet economists have paid scant attention to whether the president's tax policy communication affects economic activity via such a sentiment channel. This is surprising, given the reemerging interest in the idea of animal spirits (Keynes, 1936) in economic research (Akerlof and Shiller, 2009). Blinder and Watson (2016) have recently revived the economic interest in the U.S. president by investigating why the U.S. economy has consistently performed better during Democratic than during Republican presidencies. We share this interest in the president's importance for economic outcomes and contribute to this strand of literature by analyzing whether the salience and tone of presidential tax policy speeches influence economic sentiment and, in turn, affect economic activity.

A strand of literature not only places the president at the center of the political landscape, but identifies him as an important economic actor, whose rhetoric is a powerful tool of economic leadership (see e.g., Wood et al., 2005). Eshbaugh-Soha (2013) stresses the importance of the president's rhetoric by showing that news reports, covering presidential press conferences, often use the president's words. The incumbent also has the ability to direct media attention towards issues that are on the government's political agenda (Miles, 2014). This public attention, paired with the inherent political message that presidential speeches entail, can influence the public's economic perceptions. On occasions, presidents will prioritize certain policies to convey an image of a committed leader who takes action and maintains control. This indicated leadership can promote a feeling of security, well-being, and trust among the public. In addition, presidents try to frame their public communication in ways that convey favorable images of their economic plans. In bad times, emphasis will be on efforts to improve the federal budget or stimulate economic activity. In good times, presidents try to make sure that the public attributes the economic prosperity to their legislation (De Boef and Kellstedt, 2004;Chong and Druckman, 2007). Presidential announcements may thus temporarily shift economic sentiment even if the underlying economic reality remains unchanged (Cohen and Hamman, 2003;Wood et al., 2005).

Measuring policy statements is a difficult task as it requires the quantification of written records. This typically entails two problems. First, the collection and quantification of text data is often costly in terms of time and resources. Second, narrative measures are often prone to subjectivity due to their manual compilation (DiMaggio et al., 2013;Grimmer and Stewart, 2013). To overcome these difficulties and introduce the analysis of presidential news into empirical economic research, we resort to methods from computational linguistics. In particular, we use a correlated topic model to analyze 89,843 presidential documents with regards to their tax policy content. Although the manual analysis of policy announcement effects on economic aggregates have provided insightful results in former studies (see e.g., Büchel, 2013;Falagiarda and Gregori, 2015), probabilistic topic models increasingly find their way into economic research (see e.g., Hansen and McMahon, 2016;Azqueta-Gavaldón, 2017;Larsen, 2017).

A novelty of our approach is the combination of a correlated topic model with a dictionary-based sentiment analysis. This enables us to construct a time series that measures both the salience of tax policy issues in presidential communication and the sentiment that it conveys. As the salience and tone of tax announcements most likely affect consumption and investment decisions, we first investigate the impact of presidential tax policy news on GDP and its constituents. In a subsequent analysis, we investigate the most likely transmission channel through which these announcements may affect the real economy, namely consumer sentiment. We conduct these analyses in well-established structural vector autoregression (SVAR) models for fiscal policy analysis (see e.g., Blanchard and Perotti, 2002;Leeper et al., 2013b).

Our results show that output, private consumption and investment are stimulated by positive tax policy news. By controlling for tax foresight, we reveal a significant residual tax news effect. Building on these findings, we test for a possible sentiment channel in the transmission of the positive tax news shock on economic activity by including the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index into the SVAR framework. The results show that sentiment rises in response to a positive tax policy news shock, and output increases in response to a positive sentiment shock. Our findings complement theoretical arguments for animal spirits (see e.g., Angeletos et al., 2016;Huo and Takayama, 2015), as well as findings from related empirical studies on the effects of consumer confidence and news (see e.g., Doms and Morin, 2004;Wood et al., 2005;Ahmed and Cassou, 2016).

The simultaneous conditioning on presidential tax policy news and tax foresight yields insightful results concerning the output components. Positive tax policy news stimulate private consumption stronger than private investment. The opposite is the case for expected tax changes. These have a weaker effect on private consumption compared to private investment. Whereas consumption primarily consists of small transactions for short-lived goods, investments typically entail larger transactions with a long-term perspective. Hence, it is plausible that private consumption is more prone to optimistic presidential tax policy announcements than investment (Rook and Gardner, 1993;Hausman, 2000).

Our approach is methodologically related to Larsen (2017) who combines a simpler probabilistic topic model and word counts to identify various types of uncertainty in Norwegian newspaper articles. In terms of the research question, Wood et al. (2005) are closest to our approach. They conduct human coding of optimistic and pessimistic sentences in the Public Papers of the Presidents pertaining to the general economy and unemployment, and analyze the effect of these relative sentence counts on GPD and unemployment.

This paper provides several innovations and advancements in regards to existing studies. First, we apply a state of the art probabilistic topic model, which has several advantages over conventional word count approaches. According to DiMaggio et al. (2013), probabilistic topic models are especially suitable for text analyses as they can process large collections of data (automated), generate a reproducible data set for other researchers (explicit), without providing prior information on the structure of the corpus (inductive). They further allow terms to vary in meaning across different contexts. Instead of a binary classification decision based solely on the occurrence of a certain keyword, the correlated topic model provides a probability for each document, indicating to what extent a speech is about tax policy issues. This allows for a more subtle and precise classification of speeches and results in a continuous tax policy news measure. Second, instead of compiling our own word list, we apply a well-established dictionary that has been especially developed for sentiment analyses within political contexts (Young and Soroka, 2012). Third, rather than looking at a broad economic theme, we focus on tax relevant statements, as tax policy changes entail prospects of changing disposable income and thus have a direct effect on economic sentiment. Finally, we embed the presidential tax news measure in a structural VAR model that captures the dynamic effects of fiscal policy for the U.S.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 explains the correlated topic model and our data compilation. Section 3 presents the econometric framework as well as our empirical results. Section 4 concludes.

paragraphs: Probabilistic topic models are a class of unsupervised machine learning algorithms that enable the automatic detection of themes in large collections of written documents. An important feature of these models is that hidden topics can be revealed without additional document information, such as topic labels, classifications or annotations. The most prominent and widely used algorithm is latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) by Blei et al. (2003).

Most probabilistic topic models rely on semantic simplifications, such as the bag of words assumption, to discover the latent themes within documents.foot_0 While this assumption may seem unrealistic, it proves to be highly effective when the primary goal is to uncover the thematic content of texts (Blei, 2012;Grimmer and Stewart, 2013).

Since the introduction of LDA, much progress has been made to allow for a more realistic modeling of a document's generating process. As part of this progress, Blei and Lafferty (2007) have developed the correlated topic model (CTM) which explicitly allows topics to be correlated within documents. Imagine a speech by the U.S. President discussing tax policy. Such a speech is more likely to also touch on issues concerning the federal budget than, for example, inviting a championship sports team to the White House. Thus, the CTM not only offers a more realistic representation of documents but also yields more accurate topic classifications (Blei and Lafferty, 2007).
Consider a collection of D documents, indexed by d, which consist of N words, and V unique words w, indexed by {1,…, V }.

Documents consist of K topics 𝛽 k , where each 𝛽 k is a discrete distribution over the fixed vocabulary. Each document, however, exhibits a different proportion 𝜃 d of those topics, and each word within a document is assigned to only one topic, denoted by z d,n . The CTM attempts to allocate the documents' words to as few topics as possible, while at the same time trying to assign high probabilities to only a few words within each topic. This trade-off enables the algorithm to capture the relationship of meaning between words in text corpora. 2To better grasp the intuition behind the CTM, Fig. 1 depicts its generative process as a graphical model. Repeating variables are grouped together by rectangles. White nodes are latent random variables, and shaded nodes denote observable variables. The only observable data are the documents' words (w d,n ), whereas the topic proportions (𝜃 d ), word assignments (z d,n ), and topics (𝛽 k ) are unobserved and will be inferred by the algorithm.
The corresponding probabilistic representation of the CTM's generative process can be described as follows:

-𝛽 k ∼ Dir (𝛾). K topics with dimension V are drawn from a (sparse) Dirichlet distribution, parameterized by a vector 𝛾 with 0 < 𝛾 i < 1, with i = 1,...,V. Topics are drawn only once for the entire corpus.

The logistic normal distribution can be represented by drawing a K dimensional vector of a multivariate Gaussian distribution, mapped to the K -1 topic simplex by 𝜃 d,k . The vector of the topic proportions is 𝜃 d . The covariance matrix Σ accounts for correlations between the topic proportions.
Each word in a document is assigned to only one topic, depending on the topic proportions within each document.
A word is drawn from topic z d,n with probability given in 𝛽 z d,n .

To reveal the hidden variables from given documents, the algorithm reverses the generative process, asking which hidden structure most likely generated the observed documents (Blei, 2012). We estimate the CTM via a partially collapsed variational EM algorithm, developed by Roberts et al. (2016), and implemented in the stm-package in R (Roberts et al., 2014). The starting values for the The number of unique words in the prepared corpus is 83,569.

algorithm are chosen by a robust moment-based estimator as suggested by Arora et al. (2013) and Roberts et al. (2016). 3The remaining task for the researcher is to choose an adequate number of topics, which we set to 100. Our choice reflects the careful examination of a trade-off: If the present number of topics is too small, topics are potentially inflated and too general for specific analyses. By the same token, setting the number of topics too large results in an over-fitting, which causes a subdivision of one topic into multiple related topics, hindering their interpretation (DiMaggio et al., 2013). Since our aim is to find the best fit for the latent topics contained in the corpus, we ran the algorithm with 50, 75, 100, and 150 topics on our corpus. We find that a calibration of 100 topics yields the best-behaved results with respect to the outlined trade-off.

Documents consist of K topics 𝛽 k , where each 𝛽 k is a discrete distribution over the fixed vocabulary. Each document, however, exhibits a different proportion 𝜃 d of those topics, and each word within a document is assigned to only one topic, denoted by z d,n . The CTM attempts to allocate the documents' words to as few topics as possible, while at the same time trying to assign high probabilities to only a few words within each topic. This trade-off enables the algorithm to capture the relationship of meaning between words in text corpora. 2To better grasp the intuition behind the CTM, Fig. 1 depicts its generative process as a graphical model. Repeating variables are grouped together by rectangles. White nodes are latent random variables, and shaded nodes denote observable variables. The only observable data are the documents' words (w d,n ), whereas the topic proportions (𝜃 d ), word assignments (z d,n ), and topics (𝛽 k ) are unobserved and will be inferred by the algorithm.

The corresponding probabilistic representation of the CTM's generative process can be described as follows:

-𝛽 k ∼ Dir (𝛾). K topics with dimension V are drawn from a (sparse) Dirichlet distribution, parameterized by a vector 𝛾 with 0 < 𝛾 i < 1, with i = 1,...,V. Topics are drawn only once for the entire corpus.

.

The logistic normal distribution can be represented by drawing a K dimensional vector of a multivariate Gaussian distribution, mapped to the K -1 topic simplex by 𝜃 d,k . The vector of the topic proportions is 𝜃 d . The covariance matrix Σ accounts for correlations between the topic proportions.

-

Each word in a document is assigned to only one topic, depending on the topic proportions within each document.

-

A word is drawn from topic z d,n with probability given in 𝛽 z d,n .

To reveal the hidden variables from given documents, the algorithm reverses the generative process, asking which hidden structure most likely generated the observed documents (Blei, 2012). We estimate the CTM via a partially collapsed variational EM algorithm, developed by Roberts et al. (2016), and implemented in the stm-package in R (Roberts et al., 2014). The starting values for the The number of unique words in the prepared corpus is 83,569.

algorithm are chosen by a robust moment-based estimator as suggested by Arora et al. (2013) and Roberts et al. (2016). 3The remaining task for the researcher is to choose an adequate number of topics, which we set to 100. Our choice reflects the careful examination of a trade-off: If the present number of topics is too small, topics are potentially inflated and too general for specific analyses. By the same token, setting the number of topics too large results in an over-fitting, which causes a subdivision of one topic into multiple related topics, hindering their interpretation (DiMaggio et al., 2013). Since our aim is to find the best fit for the latent topics contained in the corpus, we ran the algorithm with 50, 75, 100, and 150 topics on our corpus. We find that a calibration of 100 topics yields the best-behaved results with respect to the outlined trade-off.
sections.preview_head: Probabilistic topic models are a class of unsupervised machine learning algorithms that enable the automatic detection of themes in large collections of written documents. An important feature of these models is that hidden topics can be revealed without additional document information, such as topic labels, classifications or annotations. The most prominent and widely used algorithm is latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) by Blei et al. (2003).

paragraphs: Our source for presidential communication is The American Presidency Project, an online resource dedicated to the study of the U.S. Presidency by Woolley and Peters (2017). It contains an extensive collection of presidential transcripts, such as presidential speeches, radio addresses, State of the Union addresses, inaugural speeches, press conferences and statements from the White House.

We use an algorithm to collect each document from January 1945 to December 2016 together with metadata. For each speech, we record the date, the title and the speaker, along with an ID that uniquely identifies each document. The corpus contains 89,843 documents, comprising around 90 million words. To reduce complexity, we pre-process all documents according to standard routines in computational linguistics. We separate words by delimiting characters such as hyphens or apostrophes. We also remove words with less than three letters, words that belong to a standard stoplist (i.e., articles, conjunctions or common functional words such as the, also, or because), and words that occur only once in the corpus. Individual characters and numbers are removed as well. As a result, we obtain a condensed corpus of 83,569 unique words (V) that appear 31,620,652 times in total. Table 1 presents descriptive statistics for the raw and the pre-processed corpus.

sections.header_raw: Measuring the salience of tax policy news
sections.header_norm: measuring the salience of tax policy news
paragraphs: Table 2 gives an excerpt of eight topics revealed by the CTM consisting of the 20 most probable words for each topic in descending order. Since the CTM finds topics solely based on relational word occurrences, each topic is only given a number from 1 to 100 (topic order is irrelevant). It is up to the researcher to make meaning of each topic based on the coherence of words it contains with high probability (DiMaggio et al., 2013). Accordingly, we add the following headlines to the eight topics depicted in Table 2: tax policy, environment, legislation, political parties, health care, crime, drugs, and labor market.

With our research question in mind, we identify topic 84 as the tax policy topic, as it has highest probability for the words tax, budget, cut, spend, etc. To analyze the salience of this topic over time, we construct a monthly time series and refer to it as tax policy news measure (TNM). The TNM is defined as the average over the topic proportions of topic 84 for each month, normalized by dividing each value by the maximum realization of the time series.

sections.header_raw: Measuring sentiment in tax policy news
sections.header_norm: measuring sentiment in tax policy news
paragraphs: As presidential speeches resemble a political instrument, presidents often use them to convey a favorable image of their economic policy (Wood et al., 2005;De Boef and Kellstedt, 2004). To capture this notion, we combine the TNM with a sentiment analysis based on the Lexicoder Sentiment Dictionary, which has been especially developed for sentiment analyses within political contexts (Young and Soroka, 2012). Similar to Hansen and McMahon (2016), we measure the relative difference of positive and negative words in each document by: ) are word patterns indicating a negative (positive) word preceded by a negation, and w total d,n the total number of words within each document. As a consequence, 𝜙 d is placed in the interval [-1, 1], where 𝜙 d > 0 indicates a positive tone and 𝜙 d < 0 a negative tone. To capture the sentiment of tax policy related documents, we use a similar approach as Larsen (2017) and multiply each proportion of topic 84 with the document's sentiment score:

(2) The table presents a selection of eight topics from the 100 topics discovered by the CTM. For each topic, we show the 20 most probable words in descending order. Topic labels are assigned from visual inspection of the words contained in each topic.

This new measure not only indicates how much a document is about topic 84 (i.e., tax policy), but also how optimistic or pessimistic it is conveyed. We compute monthly averages of 𝜑 d and normalize them to the interval [-1, 1]. In the following we refer to this time series as tax policy news sentiment measure (TNSM). We conducted several unit-root tests to validate that the TNSM is stationary.foot_3 Fig. 2 depicts the normalized TNM (upper panel) and TNSM (lower panel). It shows that both time series co-move, although differing in the magnitude for certain spikes. While the TNM rises solely due to a high prevalence of tax policy speeches, the TNSM spikes through a combination of high topic proportions and a positive tone within those speeches. More precisely, a high value of the TNSM would indicate a month in which many tax news arise with a predominantly positive tone. A low value of the TNSM would indicate the opposite: a month with relatively pessimistic tax news.

To validate the TNSM in regards to its reflection of positive and negative sentiments, we group the realizations for the TNSM according to whether a month falls into a documented tax cut or tax increase legislation.foot_4 Fig. 3 shows the dispersion of the TNSM realizations conditional on the tax legislation. As expected, the TNSM is higher during periods of tax cut legislation than during periods of tax increases. 6We stress at this point that the TNSM does not measure the direction of tax changes (i.e. tax cut or tax increase) as the president's communication of a tax change can differ from its actual implementation. For example, even though the Deficit Reduction Act of 1984 included a major tax increase, President Reagan publicly opposed tax increases beforehand. In his weekly radio address on January 21, 1984, he openly discussed the federal budget while favoring spending cuts over tax increases. Six months later, however, the bill was signed with almost no public notice as it included the shut down of several tax loopholes, effectively increasing taxes. 7 This example shows that Reagan's promises to keep taxes low might have shifted economic sentiments even though the economic reality was a pending tax increase.

This anecdotal evidence corroborates a characteristic we find in our data. Of all speeches with high tax policy content (𝜃 d,84 > 0.15), 557 fall into tax cut episodes, whereas only 267 fall into tax increase legislations. The result remains robust for different thresholds for 𝜃 d,84 . It underlines the notion that tax cuts are more often and openly discussed by U.S. presidents than tax increases. Communication around tax increases is often deliberately framed as a necessity to bring down the budget deficit. The TNSM captures both narratives.foot_7

paragraphs: Having constructed the TNSM and validated it for its thematic adequacy, we are interested in its effect on the macroeconomy. For this purpose, we first estimate the effect of presidential tax policy news on macroeconomic aggregates, and then deepen this analysis by looking at the most likely transmission channel through which the salience and tone of presidential tax policy speeches affect the macroeconomy: consumer sentiment.

sections.paper_id: Dybowski and Adämmer - 2018 - The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication Evidence from a correlated topic model
sections.header_raw: Uncovering the residual effect of presidential tax policy news
sections.header_norm: uncovering the residual effect of presidential tax policy news
paragraphs: The empirical literature on fiscal policy stresses the necessity to control for fiscal foresight in the conditioning variables of a VAR to recover the true (unexpected) structural tax shock (see e.g., Leeper et al., 2013a). Empirical studies accounting for foresight typically find a temporary expansionary effect in economic activity, originating from the expected tax increase. These results are suggestive of inter-temporal substitution effects, triggered during the pre-implementation phase of announced tax increases (see e.g., Leeper et al., 2013b;Mertens and Ravn, 2012).

To adequately measure the effect of (positive) presidential tax policy news, we thus have to account for tax expectations. Aside from recovering the (unexpected) structural tax shock, controlling for tax foresight helps us to disentangle the direct effects of expected tax changes from the ancillary effect, stemming from optimistic presidential remarks on fiscal policy in general and on tax policy in particular.

A vast literature shows that the yield spread between tax exempt municipal bonds and taxable government bonds informs on market expectations of future tax changes (see e.g., Poterba, 1989;Fortune, 1996;Kueng, 2011). This yield spread is called implicit tax rate, and, by definition, increases with market expectations of tax increases and vice versa (Leeper et al., 2012).

paragraphs: Turning to the empirical analysis, we build on the SVAR framework of Blanchard and Perotti (2002) that uses quarterly data on total tax revenues (T t ), government spending (G t ) and output (X t ) to investigate the dynamic effects of fiscal policy in the U.S. economy. To isolate the residual effect of presidental tax news (N t ), we follow Leeper et al. (2013b) and include the implicit tax rate (S t ) to control for future expected tax changes.

We next investigate intermediary effects and a likely transmission channel that help shed more light on the observed effect on output.

sections.paper_id: Dybowski and Adämmer - 2018 - The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication Evidence from a correlated topic model
sections.header_raw: The effects of presidential tax news on consumption and investment
sections.header_norm: the effects of presidential tax news on consumption and investment
paragraphs: The roots of economic activity rest on millions of consumers making billions of consumption and investment decisions every day. Undeniably, most such decisions are directly influenced by economic realities such as household's disposable income. Yet a substantial fraction may be driven indirectly by economic perceptions (Wood et al., 2005). It is precisely these perceptions that potentially respond to information and evaluations by the president in regards to tax and fiscal policy matters. In sum, these decisions amount to macroeconomic aggregates of private investment and consumption. As both components make up a combined 83 percent of total U.S. income, movements in these aggregates would explain the bulk of the effect in GDP recorded in section 3.2. 11 Fig. 4. Impulse responses of GDP to a unit increase in (unanticipated) taxes, tax policy sentiment news (TNSM), and anticipated taxes (implicit tax rate). Shaded regions denote one standard deviation confidence intervals, based on Monte Carlo simulation with 1000 iterations (assuming normality).

To formally test this notion, we follow Blanchard and Perotti (2002) and augment the SVAR in (6) sequentially with these two components (X c ), which are ordered after GDP in the system. 12 The additional identifying restrictions encompass that there is no immediate impact of the output component on tax revenues (a X c = 0) and government spending (b X c = 0). The output component itself does not respond contemporaneously to tax policy news (f N = 0) and the implicit tax rate (f S = 0). Finally, as GDP comprises private consumption and investment, it is assumed that c X c = 0 and f X = 0 so as to allow 𝜀 X t and 𝜀 X c t to be correlated (see Blanchard and Perotti, 2002). Under these restrictions, the relation between reduced-form residuals and structural innovations becomes

(7) Figs. 5 and 6 depict the results of model ( 7) for private consumption and investment, respectively. The left columns in both figures depict the estimated responses of GDP. It is reassuring that the inclusion of private consumption and investment does not alter the results for GDP (compare to Fig. 4).

Following the intuition stated previously, we are interested in the effects on private consumption and investment depicted in the right columns of Figs. 5 and 6. The effects of an (unexpected) tax increase on private consumption and investment are in line with results of Blanchard and Perotti (2002). We find a clear negative effect on private consumption after an (unexpected) increase in taxes, which is consistent with a Keynesian model, where increased taxes lower disposable income, resulting in lower consumption. We also find a negative effect on private investment after an (unexpected) increase of taxes. In a Keynesian model, the response of investment hinges on the relative strength of the effect on output and the interest rate. A decrease of private investment, as we observe it, is justifiable if the output effect dominates the interest rate effect. 13Turning to the focal point of our analysis, namely the responses to positive presidential tax policy news, we find a pronounced positive effect on private consumption over the entire horizon. Following a positive tax news shock, private consumption increases up to a peak effect of around 0.07 after 7 quarters, before the effect attenuates towards the end of the horizon. A similar picture arises for private investment. Although there seems to be no immediate response after a positive tax news shock, private investment increases over the medium horizon to around 0.04 before the effect slowly phases out. The combined magnitudes of the responses of consumption and investment compare well to the overall effect we observe on GDP. Controlling for the direction of expected tax changes does not render the effects of the residual tax news shock insignificant. Comparing the results of presidential tax news and expected tax changes on the output components reveals an interesting pattern. Notice that private consumption responds more pronounced to a positive shock in presidential tax policy news than private investment. The reverse holds true for the responses to expected tax changes. Private consumption only responds slightly positively and even turns negative towards the end of the horizon. In contrast to this, private investment clearly responds positively and pronounced to an expected tax change. These results reflect the different characteristics of consumption and investment goods: if positive presidential news about tax policy trigger optimism, we would expect consumption to be more prone to such a stimulus than investments. Conceivably, investment decisions are more likely based on unemotional (rational) economic assessments than consumption. For the latter, emotions or affective states are often regarded as triggers for impulse buying. According to research in consumer behavior, impulse buying accounts for a substantial volume of goods bought across a broad range of consumption products every year (see, e.g. Hausman, 2000). Rook and Gardner (1993) find that positive moods such as pleasure, carefree or excitement are the most conductive for impulse buying. Such mood states are potentially triggered by optimistic presidential announcements concerning tax policy.

Following the intuition stated previously, we are interested in the effects on private consumption and investment depicted in the right columns of Figs. 5 and 6. The effects of an (unexpected) tax increase on private consumption and investment are in line with results of Blanchard and Perotti (2002). We find a clear negative effect on private consumption after an (unexpected) increase in taxes, which is consistent with a Keynesian model, where increased taxes lower disposable income, resulting in lower consumption. We also find a negative effect on private investment after an (unexpected) increase of taxes. In a Keynesian model, the response of investment hinges on the relative strength of the effect on output and the interest rate. A decrease of private investment, as we observe it, is justifiable if the output effect dominates the interest rate effect. 13Turning to the focal point of our analysis, namely the responses to positive presidential tax policy news, we find a pronounced positive effect on private consumption over the entire horizon. Following a positive tax news shock, private consumption increases up to a peak effect of around 0.07 after 7 quarters, before the effect attenuates towards the end of the horizon. A similar picture arises for private investment. Although there seems to be no immediate response after a positive tax news shock, private investment increases over the medium horizon to around 0.04 before the effect slowly phases out. The combined magnitudes of the responses of consumption and investment compare well to the overall effect we observe on GDP. Controlling for the direction of expected tax changes does not render the effects of the residual tax news shock insignificant. Comparing the results of presidential tax news and expected tax changes on the output components reveals an interesting pattern. Notice that private consumption responds more pronounced to a positive shock in presidential tax policy news than private investment. The reverse holds true for the responses to expected tax changes. Private consumption only responds slightly positively and even turns negative towards the end of the horizon. In contrast to this, private investment clearly responds positively and pronounced to an expected tax change. These results reflect the different characteristics of consumption and investment goods: if positive presidential news about tax policy trigger optimism, we would expect consumption to be more prone to such a stimulus than investments. Conceivably, investment decisions are more likely based on unemotional (rational) economic assessments than consumption. For the latter, emotions or affective states are often regarded as triggers for impulse buying. According to research in consumer behavior, impulse buying accounts for a substantial volume of goods bought across a broad range of consumption products every year (see, e.g. Hausman, 2000). Rook and Gardner (1993) find that positive moods such as pleasure, carefree or excitement are the most conductive for impulse buying. Such mood states are potentially triggered by optimistic presidential announcements concerning tax policy.
sections.preview_head: The roots of economic activity rest on millions of consumers making billions of consumption and investment decisions every day. Undeniably, most such decisions are directly influenced by economic realities such as household's disposable income. Yet a substantial fraction may be driven indirectly by economic perceptions (Wood et al., 2005).

sections.paper_id: Dybowski and Adämmer - 2018 - The economic effects of U.S. presidential tax communication Evidence from a correlated topic model
paragraphs: Modern economies are characterized by a close interaction between politics and economics. As the central actor on the political stage, the president holds a unique position to influence economic perceptions through his policy communication. While previous fiscal policy studies have argued that presidential speeches can lead to shifts in tax expectations, the analysis on the effect of presidential tax communication on economic activity has been neglected thus far. We fill this gap in the literature by combining a probabilistic topic model and a dictionary-based sentiment analysis to construct a measure that captures the salience and tone of U.S. presidential tax communication since 1945.

The econometric analyses show that positive tax policy news by the president stimulate private consumption, investment and output. By controlling for tax foresight, we ensure that we do not capture a confounded tax expectation effect but a residual effect stemming from (positive) presidential tax policy news. Our finding that private consumption responds more pronounced than investment to this positive tax news shock suggests that the effect is driven by sentiments.

We further analyze the most important transmission channel through which tax policy communication may influence economic activity, namely consumer sentiment. Our results show that a positive tax policy news shock increases consumer sentiment, which in turn stimulates economic activity. In addition, the direct effect of positive tax policy news on output is less pronounced in the model with consumer sentiment. This suggests that confidence is an important transmission channel through which presidential news affect economic activity.
